mappings.rationaleAI: Behavioural ecology covers optimal foraging (food getting), sexual selection and mate choice (reproduction-related behaviour), kin selection, cooperation and altruism (social behaviour dynamics), and the distinction between social and nonsocial animal groups.

Pheromones are chemical signals used by animals for communication including sexual attraction, alarm signaling, territory marking, and social organisation -- directly relevant to animal communication.

Echolocation in bats and dolphins demonstrates sophisticated sensory-guided food-getting behaviour, avoidance of obstacles, and a form of biological communication -- relevant to food getting, avoidance behaviour, and animal communication.

Slime moulds solve spatial problems and navigate mazes without a nervous system, demonstrating complex problem solving and collective behaviour that blurs the line between individual and group organisms.

Cephalopods demonstrate remarkable intelligence, complex problem solving, color-changing communication via chromatophores, and sophisticated predatory behaviour -- relevant to food getting, problem solving, and animal communication.

The infant brain covers critical periods for language acquisition, the role of attachment in development, and the nature-nurture interplay in learning -- directly relevant to associative learning, imprinting, and language learning.

Altruism examines cooperation and prosocial behaviour across human and animal societies through kin selection, reciprocal altruism, and game theory -- directly relevant to the range and dynamics of social behaviour.

Coral spawning involves coordinated group reproduction behaviour, and coral reef ecosystems illustrate the colonial organisms that exist between individual and group life -- relevant to the distinction between social and nonsocial groups and interspecies associations.
